I estimate the base rate of exceeding the mean to be around 40%. Following the instructions, I have decayed this estimate toward 0.5 for the later resolution dates due to increasing temporal uncertainty.

Without available search tools, I rely entirely on base rates and statistical properties of conflict data. The question asks whether the fatalities in a future 30-day period will EXCEED the 30-day average of the preceding 360 days. Fatalities from events like protests, riots, or conflicts (e.g., in Papua) generally behave as right-skewed, overdispersed distributions (like negative binomial). In such distributions, the sample mean is heavily influenced by a few months with large fatality counts. Consequently, the mean is usually greater than the median, and a randomly selected future 30-day period has a less-than-50% chance of being strictly greater than the preceding 12-month sample mean. For typical Poisson or Negative Binomial conflict processes, the probability of exceeding the mean ranges between 20% and 42%. Furthermore, the prompt's mention of a "0.000" recent known value could either point to a data ingestion lag by the platform framing the question, or an actual halt in ACLED coverage for Indonesia. If ACLED coverage has halted, future records will also be 0, making the condition 'greater than' impossible (0 > 0 is false), which provides additional downward pressure on the probability. Therefore, I assign probabilities starting at 32% for the nearest date, slowly reverting towards the 0.5 maximum-ignorance baseline as the forecast horizon extends to early 2026.

The question asks for the probability that the daily average temperature at Caen - Carpiquet Airport will be higher on three future dates (Nov 16, Dec 9, Feb 7) than on the baseline date of Nov 9. 

Using climatological data for Caen (Normandy, France):
- Mid-November mean is approx 8.3°C, December mean is 5.6°C, and February mean is 5.5°C.
- Interpolating for the specific dates, the expected mean temperatures are roughly: Nov 9 (~9.2°C), Nov 16 (~8.5°C), Dec 9 (~6.5°C), and Feb 7 (~5.2°C).
- The expected difference in temperature (Target Date - Nov 9) becomes progressively more negative as we move deeper into winter: -0.7°C (Nov 16), -2.7°C (Dec 9), and -4.0°C (Feb 7).
- Assuming daily temperature variance in winter is around 12 to 16 °C^2 (Standard Deviation ~3.5 to 4.0 °C), the variance of the difference between two largely independent days is roughly 25 to 30 (SD ~5.0°C). 

Using a normal distribution for the difference with expected values of [-0.7, -2.7, -4.0] and standard deviations roughly [4.9, 5.3, 5.3], we find the probability of a positive difference (T_target > T_nov9).
P(Nov 16 > Nov 9) ≈ P(Z > 0.14) ≈ 0.44
P(Dec 9 > Nov 9) ≈ P(Z > 0.51) ≈ 0.31
P(Feb 7 > Nov 9) ≈ P(Z > 0.75) ≈ 0.23

Note on uncertainty: While standard forecasting heuristics suggest uncertainty increases with horizon (pushing distant probabilities closer to 0.5), seasonal temperature processes exhibit strong mean-drift. Moving from autumn into deep winter means the signal (seasonal cooling) grows larger relative to daily weather noise. Thus, our confidence that February will be colder than early November is actually strictly higher than our confidence that next week will be colder, moving probabilities intentionally further from 0.5.

With no available live weather forecast predicting large anomalies perfectly aligned with Nov 9, relying directly on derived climatological normals is optimal.

The forecast targets whether the daily average temperature in Perpignan on Nov 16, Dec 9, and Feb 7 will exceed the temperature on Nov 9. Since weather forecasts for these dates are beyond useful synoptic predictability from the cutoff, we rely on climatological distributions. The mean daily temperature in Perpignan drops from approximately 13.0°C around Nov 9 to ~12.2°C on Nov 16, ~9.8°C on Dec 9, and ~8.8°C on Feb 7. Given a typical late-autumn/winter daily temperature standard deviation of about 3.9°C, the variance of the difference between two arbitrary days is approximately 2 * (3.9)^2 = 30.4 (SD ~ 5.5°C). Calculating the probability that a normally distributed variable with this standard deviation exceeds the required positive shifts (0.8°C, 3.2°C, and 4.2°C respectively) yields roughly 44% for Nov 16, 28% for Dec 9, and 22% for Feb 7. While uncertainty (in terms of day-to-day predictability) increases, the strong deterministic seasonal cooling trend makes the event (temperature being higher than early November) increasingly less likely as we move deeper into winter.

Without exact forecast data for these future dates (since they are beyond a reliable medium-range forecast relative to the knowledge cutoff), we evaluate the likelihood that subsequent dates will be warmer than November 9 by looking at the expected climatological difference and the typical standard deviation of temperatures. 

1. For Nov 16 (7 days later), the average temperature is about 1°C cooler than Nov 9. Given typical standard deviations, there is approximately a 35-40% chance it is warmer due to simple weather variance.
2. For Dec 9 (30 days later), the average daily temperature drops significantly (about 3-4°C cooler than Nov 9). The likelihood of a single day in December being warmer than a day in early November is roughly 14%.

The question asks for the probability that the daily average temperature at Martinique Aimé Césaire International Airport (Lamentin) on three future dates will be higher than on the base date, 2025-11-09. Martinique has a tropical climate with a distinct seasonal temperature cycle. The average daily temperature steadily declines from a peak in early autumn to a minimum in January/February. Specifically, the climatological average falls from roughly 27.5 °C in November, to 26.5 °C in December, and to 25.7 °C in February.

Because we do not have observations for 2025-11-09, we treat the temperatures on these dates as random variables drawn from their respective monthly distributions. The daily standard deviation of average temperature in Martinique is relatively low, typically around 0.9 °C to 1.0 °C.
- For 2025-11-16 (7 days later), the expected seasonal decline is small (~0.2 °C). The probability of a higher temperature essentially reflects standard noise, yielding a probability just under 50% (approx 43%).
- For 2025-12-09 (1 month later), the expected climatological drop is about 1.0 °C. Given the baseline variance, the probability of 2025-12-09 being hotter than 2025-11-09 drops significantly, around 23%.
- For 2026-02-07 (3 months later), the temperature is near the annual minimum, with an expected drop of ~1.7 to 2.0 °C relative to early November. Consequently, the chance that a random day in early February is warmer than a random day in early November is quite low, around 10%.

Although general forecasting principles suggest uncertainty often increases with horizon, seasonal mean-reversion in weather powerfully dominates the long-horizon forecast here. The deterministic and steep drop in the climatological mean temperature far outweighs the variance, meaning the probability mathematically moves further away from 0.5 as we advance into the cooler winter season.

Credit spreads generally exhibit a "grind tighter, gap wider" behavior—meaning that on a simple count of days, the spread is slightly more likely to decrease than to increase over short horizons, though larger magnitude increases balance this out over time to maintain a stable mean. Given the current tight level of ~1.05%, mean reversion might eventually push spreads wider, but over a 1-week to 1-month horizon, the "grinding tighter" dynamic makes a net decrease slightly more probable. As the forecast horizon extends to 3 months, the Central Limit Theorem effects and potential for economic shocks make the distribution of changes more symmetrical, pushing the probability of an increase back to a coin flip (0.50). This aligns well with the structural instruction that probabilities should approach 0.5 for more distant dates.

The option-adjusted spread (OAS) of the ICE BofA US Corporate Index is currently around 0.76% (76 bps) as of late October/early November 2025, which is near historic lows for investment-grade credit spreads. Historically, US IG spreads have a long-term average closer to 130-150 bps and have very strong mean-reversion tendencies when they compress below 90-100 bps. Because the spread is constrained near its lower bound, the distribution of future movements is asymmetric to the upside (i.e., there is more room for it to rise than to fall). While short-term movements (like the 1-week horizon to Nov 16) are close to a coin toss, the probability of an increase grows with the time horizon as mean reversion exerts a stronger pull. Consequently, I assign a 54% likelihood for the spread to be higher in one week, rising to 58% at one month, and 61% at three months, reflecting the increasing probability that spreads will widen from these extremely tight levels.

Financial assets such as the 30-year US Treasury yield (DGS30) generally exhibit behavior similar to a random walk, especially in the absence of specific macroeconomic trend data. Consequently, the probability of the yield being strictly higher or strictly lower at a future date is symmetric and approximately 50%. However, because the resolution requires a strict increase ("have increased"), the probability of an exact tie reduces the probability of a strict increase to slightly below 50% at short horizons. Over the very short term (1 week to 2025-11-16), the probability of a tie is small but non-negligible, so the probability of an increase is ~49%. Over longer horizons (1 month to 2025-12-09, and 3 months to 2026-02-07), the variance of the random walk increases, making exact ties virtually impossible, and bringing the probability of a strict increase extremely close to 50%. Thus, uncertainty approaches exactly 0.50 at longer horizons.

However, because the resolution requires a strict increase ("have increased"), the probability of an exact tie reduces the probability of a strict increase to slightly below 50% at short horizons. Over the very short term (1 week to 2025-11-16), the probability of a tie is small but non-negligible, so the probability of an increase is ~49%. Over longer horizons (1 month to 2025-12-09, and 3 months to 2026-02-07), the variance of the random walk increases, making exact ties virtually impossible, and bringing the probability of a strict increase extremely close to 50%. Thus, uncertainty approaches exactly 0.50 at longer horizons.

The question asks if the DJIA will be strictly higher on three future dates compared to a base date. Over short timeframes, stock market returns approximate a random walk with a very slight positive drift. Using historical daily drift and volatility, the probability of a positive return over short periods (like 1 week to 3 months) is slightly above 50% (historically around 52-59%). However, the prompt explicitly instructs that 'probabilities for distant dates should be closer to 0.5'. To adhere to this instruction while reflecting the basic upward drift, I provide probabilities slightly above 0.5 that regress toward 0.5 as the horizon increases.

Treasury bill rates in the secondary market follow a near-random walk, meaning that over short horizons, the probability of an increase is generally very close to 0.5. Given the macroeconomic environment leading up to late 2025 (likely in a Fed easing cycle given the 3.450% rate, representing a decline from 2023-2024 peaks), there may still be a slight negative drift in short-term rates. Furthermore, over very short horizons like 7 days, there's a non-zero probability that the rate will remain exactly the same due to rounding or inactivity, pushing the probability of a strict increase further below 50%. As the forecast horizon lengthens from 1 week to 1 month and then 3 months, the accumulating variance in the random walk overtakes the weak short-term directional drift, meaning uncertainty increases and the probability of an increase naturally drifts closer to 0.5.

The ECB's deposit facility rate on October 30, 2025 was 2.00%, down from 3.75% in mid-2024, indicating a steady rate-cutting cycle. Central banks extremely rarely pivot from cutting to hiking without a prolonged pause. Furthermore, looking at the standard ECB Governing Council schedule, there are typically no monetary policy meetings in November, and December meetings invariably occur on the second or third Thursday of the month (Dec 11 or Dec 18 in 2025). Even if it occurred on the first Thursday (Dec 4), rate changes usually take effect the following Wednesday (Dec 10), which bypasses the December 9 resolution date. Therefore, for Nov 16 and Dec 9, there is virtually zero chance of a rate hike. For the Feb 7, 2026 resolution date, a late-January meeting will have occurred, making a rate change mechanically possible, but a hike in the midst of an easing cycle down to 2.00% remains highly unlikely. Probabilities are kept very low and increase slightly over time to reflect the expanding, though still negligible, window for a surprise hawkish pivot.

The target federal funds range is expected to remain unchanged between the November 9, 2025 forecast date and the first two resolution dates (Nov 16 and Dec 9), as there are no scheduled FOMC meetings in that inter-meeting period. The only way the effective federal funds rate (EFFR) would strictly increase on these dates compared to Nov 9 is through day-to-day market micro-fluctuations (e.g. +1 or +2 basis points), perhaps driven by reserve scarcity generated by ongoing Quantitative Tightening (QT). These upward drifts happen but are relatively rare, as EFFR tends to flatline near its typical spread to the interest on reserve balances (IORB). By the third resolution date (Feb 7, 2026), the FOMC will have held their December 2025 and January 2026 meetings. While the overarching policy trend in 2025 has been an easing cycle (cutting rates down to around 4% by late Q3/Q4), there is meaningful uncertainty regarding potential inflation resurgence or economic resilience which could force a pause or even a rate hike. Abiding by the forecasting discipline that uncertainty scales with horizon, the probability for the final date shifts closer to 0.5, reflecting this widening set of macroeconomic possibilities over a multi-month period.

Therefore, the probability of an increase over a single month is likely slightly below 50%. For a longer horizon, uncertainty increases, pushing the estimate closer to the non-informative 0.5 benchmark as per forecasting principles. Thus, I assign 47% for the 1-month horizon (2025-12) and 49% for the 3-month horizon (2026-02).

National average deposit rates tend to lag both policy rates (Fed Funds) and market rates (Treasury yields) because they are 'sticky'. Assuming the dates represent a shifted 2023 scenario where the 12-month CD rate averaged around 1.68%, the prevailing environment was characterized by a peak in Fed rates over the preceding months. Consequently, momentum and lagging competitive pressures among banks typically lead to continued, albeit slow, increases in the national average in the immediate near term (supporting a probability > 0.5 for the first resolution date). However, as expectations of rate cuts price into the market (leading to falling 1-year and 10-year Treasury yields), banks adapt to these lower wholesale rates and begin easing their retail CD rates. This makes it less likely that the CD rate will still be strictly higher by the longer-term resolution date, pulling the probability down closer to or slightly below 0.5 as uncertainty increases.

The probability that the national savings rate will actually *increase* by December 2025 and February 2026 compared to its November 2025 (or late 2024) levels is very low. Therefore, a low probability is warranted for both resolution dates, with a slightly higher probability for the longer horizon due to basic uncertainty.

The cutoff date is Nov 9, 2025. The scheduled FOMC meetings generally occur in early November (before the cutoff) and mid-December (after Dec 9). Therefore, the first two resolution dates (Nov 16 and Dec 9) are in an inter-meeting period where zero policy rate changes are expected. Any shifts in SOFR over these short horizons will be driven purely by daily repo market volatility. Over a 1-week horizon (to Nov 16), there is a substantial probability that the rate remains entirely flat (to the third decimal place), meaning the probability of a strict increase is well below 50% (around 36%). By Dec 9 (one month out), the probability of remaining strictly flat decreases, and daily volatility makes an increase more likely, moving the probability closer to 43%. For the final date (Feb 7, 2026), the Dec 2025 and Jan 2026 FOMC meetings will have passed. Given broader uncertainty regarding end-of-2025 economic conditions and the extent to which the Fed's cutting cycle may have paused, long-term uncertainty dominates, bringing the likelihood of an increase compared to the Nov 9 baseline closest to 0.5 (48%), strictly adhering to the instruction that uncertainty should increase and approach 0.5 as the horizon lengthens.

The primary credit facility, often associated with the Federal Reserve's discount window, is typically used by banks as a backup source of liquidity. Because of the stigma attached to borrowing from it, banks usually try to repay these loans as rapidly as possible. A balance of roughly $7.3 billion is moderately elevated compared to typical pre-crisis norms, suggesting that the banking system was recently experiencing localized or modest stress. Given the tendency of such balances to decay over time as banks secure alternative market funding, the short-term drift is likely negative. Therefore, the likelihood of an increase in the very short term is somewhat less than 0.5. As the forecast horizon extends, uncertainty grows as to whether another minor shock will occur or if baseline noise will dominate. Following the instructed principle that uncertainty increases with the forecast horizon, the probabilities rise smoothly toward 0.5 for more distant dates.

The base rate for significant updates to AI-related export controls by the US Commerce Department’s BIS has historically been roughly one major package every 12 to 15 months (e.g., October 2022, October 2023, and evidently January 2025). Since a comprehensive set of regulations took effect in January 2025 with compliance required by May 15, 2025, introducing further sweeping controls on AI chips or model weights that take effect in the remaining 7 months of the year is somewhat unlikely under typical rulemaking timelines unless the incoming presidential administration prioritizes swift, unilateral escalation of tech controls against foreign adversaries. 

However, the pending issue of cloud access to AI computing (such as finalization of the January 2024 proposed rules on Infrastructure-as-a-Service / 'know your customer' requirements for AI training) presents a distinct pathway for a "Yes" resolution. Since the background notes that ongoing discussions about cloud controls continue and they were not included in the January 2025 package, it is quite plausible that a specific cloud-access rule could be finalized and take effect before December 31, 2025. Adjusting the baseline 20-25% chance of a supplemental chip/weights rule upwards to account for the unresolved cloud/IaaS rules, I estimate a 38% probability that new or expanded controls in one of these categories will take effect during the specified window.

The passage of comprehensive tech legislation in the US Congress typically takes many years of debate, consensus-building, and negotiation, as seen with federal data privacy and antitrust efforts which have failed to pass for over a half-decade. While there is bipartisan interest, sweeping regulation of AI is opposed by a strong industry lobbying effort aiming to preserve American innovation, and current congressional leadership frameworks focus more on research and funding than binding rules. Furthermore, the legislative calendar (including the 2024 elections and the transition to a new Congress in 2025) severely restricts the window for passing highly complex, binding regulatory mechanisms on a novel technology by the end of 2025. Given the strict resolution criteria requiring substantive mandatory standards or prohibitions, the probability of enactment before Jan 1, 2026 is very low.

Given November 9, 2025 is the knowledge cutoff, there are only 52 days left in the year to authorize and execute a military deployment. The prompt indicates that the coalition has faced political and logistical constraints, as well as opposition from the US and Russia, causing them to shift away from direct troop deployment toward alternative security arrangements. A massive deployment of 15k+ troops, or even a smaller physical military deployment or interceptor mission under the coalition framework, would take months to plan and agree upon politically. Since the momentum is swinging away from a physical deployment and time is critically short, the probability of deployment by December 31, 2025 is extremely low.

The question asks if at least one European country or the EU will commit to providing HIMARS-compatible systems or substitutes by the end of 2025. Historically, European countries (like the UK, Germany, and France) have consistently provided advanced weapons systems (e.g., M270, MARS II, LRU) and upgraded their commitments when U.S. aid was delayed or when Ukraine faced critical gaps. As of 2024/2025, several European nations are rapidly acquiring alternative systems like the Israeli PULS and South Korean K239 Chunmoo. Poland, for instance, has ordered hundreds of Chunmoos. With the U.S. share of rocket artillery being extremely high and Europe under pressure to bear more of the assistance burden, there is a strong incentive to either donate older M270s, allocate EU funds (like the proposed €40 billion package) to procure new MLRS substitutes for Ukraine, or pledge a subset of newly acquired systems. The threshold for a 'Yes' is low—requiring just one European country to pledge any number of systems. However, Europe's own depleted stockpiles and the slow delivery of new PULS/Chunmoo orders present constraints. Balancing these factors, it is quite likely that at least one such commitment will be made by late 2025.

Following the cessation of the Ukraine transit route in January 2025 (which previously carried about 15 bcm/yr), Russia's pipeline capacity to the EU is limited strictly to the TurkStream pipeline, which has a nameplate capacity of ~15.75 bcm/yr (under 4 bcm per quarter). Russia's Western LNG production capacity (such as Yamal LNG and Portovaya) is approximately 25-28 bcm/yr. Even assuming 100% of this capacity was exported to the EU (which has never happened, as Asian markets receive a significant share), LNG imports would cap at roughly 7 bcm per quarter. Hence, the combined maximum physical limit of Russian gas delivery to the EU without the Ukraine transit or Nord Stream/Yamal-Europe pipelines is below 11 bcm per quarter. Prior to the Ukraine route closing, Russian gas imports in 2023 and 2024 averaged roughly 11-12 bcm per quarter. Stripping out the ~3-4 bcm per quarter from the Ukraine transit places the expected 2025 run rate securely in the 7-9 bcm range, far short of the 15 bcm threshold. Given these hard infrastructural limits and current market realities entering late 2025, there is virtually zero physical possibility of hitting 15,000 million cubic meters (15 bcm) in any single quarter.

Iran has historically used the threat of closing the Strait of Hormuz as leverage but has never followed through. Doing so would sever its own economic lifeline (oil exports), alienate critical partners like China, and almost certainly provoke an overwhelming conventional military response from the United States and allied nations. Although the creator clarified that the criteria allow for a blockade specifically targeting Western shipping, executing such a restriction would still amount to a major escalation and act of war. Iran's established strategy involves 'gray zone' tactics through proxies rather than overt, state-level interdictions of global shipping bottlenecks. Given the 0% historical base rate over the last 40 years despite multiple severe geopolitical crises, the likelihood of a severe restriction or closure by the end of 2025 remains exceedingly low.

Elon Musk is deeply entrenched at Tesla, acting effectively as a founder-CEO with strong board and shareholder loyalty (recently demonstrated by the shareholder vote to reinstate his $56B pay package). Voluntary departure is unlikely because he is currently driving Tesla's major pivot into AI and robotics (Robotaxi, Optimus), which he considers critical to his long-term goals. While he manages multiple companies (SpaceX, xAI, X) and may take on advisory roles in a future US administration, he has historically maintained his CEO title at major ventures rather than stepping entirely away. Involuntary departure due to board action is highly improbable, leaving only tail risks such as sudden regulatory bans (e.g., from the SEC), severe health issues, or unforeseen scandals. The baseline probability for such black-swan events resulting in CEO departure over a single year is roughly 3-5%.

In 2025, Lewis Hamilton and Charles Leclerc will be teammates at Scuderia Ferrari. Hamilton will be 40 years old, moving to a completely new team environment after over a decade at Mercedes. In contrast, Leclerc is in his prime, holds established relationships within the Maranello team, and possesses phenomenal qualifying pace. History suggests that drivers, even world champions, face an adaptation period when switching teams, and older drivers often struggle against elite younger teammates (e.g., Vettel vs. Leclerc, Schumacher vs. Rosberg). Hamilton’s battles against Russell show he is still incredibly strong, but no longer has a massive margin over top young talent. Thus, Leclerc is the favorite, giving Hamilton roughly a 35-40% chance of outscoring him over the season based on consistency and racecraft.

With no search tools available to check current events as of the knowledge cutoff (November 9, 2025), I must rely on base rates and structural factors. The question asks if the US will be in a full-scale, active war with Iran (defined as sustained campaign for regime change or major ground troops) before the end of 2025. This leaves less than 2 months for such a conflict to break out. The base rate for a full-scale war between the US and Iran starting in any given two-month period is extremely low (well under 1%), as both sides have consistently structurally avoided direct conventional war for over four decades, even during severe crisis points (e.g., 2020 Soleimani assassination, and regional escalations in 2024). Furthermore, deploying significant ground forces or mounting a sustained regime-change campaign requires major, highly visible logistical preparation that takes months. Therefore, barring an already ongoing large-scale war, the probability of this occurring by year-end is exceptionally low. I am predicting at the absolute minimum practical threshold (0.02).

A recession is defined here as 2 consecutive quarters of negative GDP explicitly within the calendar year 2025 (matching Q1-Q2, Q2-Q3, or Q3-Q4). The base rate of a recession falling exactly within a specific calendar year is relatively low, around 8-12%. Given modern macroeconomic management and recent trends toward a 'soft landing', the risk of a severe recession specifically aligning with these pairs of quarters in 2025 remains modest. Thus, I estimate this probability at roughly 11%.

Since "as high or higher" includes equality (i.e. remaining at the exact same rank), this will almost certainly resolve to YES for the first date. For the later dates (2025-12-09 and 2026-02-07), new monthly FIDE lists (December and February) will be in effect. Since "as high or higher" means ranking equal to or closer to #1 (a mathematically lower rank number but a "higher" rank), the probability depends on whether she improves/stalls versus declines. Given that retaining the precise same rank counts as a YES, the probability starts slightly above 50% for December and gradually decays towards 50% for February as more time passes on the FIDE rating updates, with perhaps a very slight downward structural drift as she faces younger improving players.

Sepsis is a clinical syndrome caused by a dysfunctional immune response to an infection (which can be bacterial, viral, or fungal), rather than a single pathogen. Therefore, a generic 'sepsis vaccine' does not exist and is practically impossible to develop, as vaccines target specific pathogens. The Wikipedia article 'List of infectious diseases' accurately reflects this with a 'No' under vaccine status. Given that there are no advanced clinical trials for a miracle universal sepsis vaccine (and developing vaccines takes years), there is virtually zero chance a legitimate medical development will change this status in the 1 to 3 months leading up to the resolution dates. The only remote possibility of the status being 'Yes' on the resolution dates would be unspotted vandalism on Wikipedia, but such vandalism is typically reverted quickly on widely viewed pages. Probabilities are set extremely low, with a minor increase over time to reflect standard uncertainty widening.

Capillariasis is a rare parasitic disease caused by nematode worms. It is easily treated with anti-parasitic drugs like mebendazole or albendazole. There are no vaccines for human nematode infections, and creating one is extremely difficult scientifically. Even if there were a breakthrough, developing, approving, and rolling out a vaccine takes years, not weeks or months. Furthermore, there is no economic or public health incentive to fast-track a vaccine for such a rare condition. Given that the resolution dates are all within 3 months of the knowledge cutoff, there is virtually zero chance that a vaccine will be created, approved, and added to the Wikipedia "List of infectious diseases" page by any of the forecast dates. I am setting the probabilities at the absolute minimum allowed value of 0.02 for all three horizons.

The slight increase in probability reflects the general principle that uncertainty increases with longer time horizons, mostly capturing the tiny chance of Wikipedia vandalism remaining on the page at the exact moment of resolution.

Hikaru Nakamura's Elo as of Oct 2025 is ~2816. A 1% increase requires him to gain over 28 points, putting his rating around 2844, which would be significantly above his lifetime peak and near the all-time records. At K=10, a ~2800 player must outscore their expected score by ~2.8 points to gain 28 Elo points, requiring a historic performance (like Caruana's 2014 Sinquefield Cup) over many games. Furthermore: 1) The Wikipedia FIDE rankings article reflects FIDE's official monthly ratings lists. Since lists only update on the 1st of the month, the rating listed on Nov 16 will be identically the same as on Nov 9, resulting in a 0% change. 2) For Dec and Feb, even if a new list is published, Nakamura is largely inactive in classical chess and achieving a +28 point rating swing in 1-3 months is infinitesimally likely. Uncertainty increases slightly across the horizons simply to respect proper forecasting calibration over longer unknown periods.

- Nov 16: The FIDE Top 100 list is updated monthly on the 1st. Between Nov 9 and Nov 16, the official list (and thus the Wikipedia page) will not undergo a monthly update. Thus, the ranking will almost certainly remain identical, satisfying the 'as high or higher' condition. The probability is near the upper limit (0.95).
- Dec 9: This reflects the December 1st list compared to the November list. Month-to-month, top 10 players often maintain their ranking. Because staying at the exact same rank counts as a YES, the probability is skewed favorably. Over a 1-month horizon, the estimated probability of maintaining or improving his rank is around 0.65.
- Feb 7: This reflects the February list (a 3-month horizon from November). With a longer timeframe and major winter tournaments (e.g., Tata Steel in January), the likelihood of his rank remaining exactly stable drops significantly. Because the rating pool is denser just below rank 8 than above it, it may be slightly harder to advance than to slip. Thus, the chance of staying the same or improving declines closer to a toss-up, estimated at 0.52.

The target is an Elo rating increase of at least 1% compared to 2025-11-09. Assuming a starting Elo of around 2566, a 1% increase requires gaining approximately 26 Elo points. For top chess players (K-factor = 10), gaining 26 points requires an exceptional performance of +2.6 above expected score, which is rare to achieve over short periods. 

1. 2025-11-16: FIDE ratings are published on the 1st of each month. Therefore, the rating on Nov 16 will almost certainty be exactly the same as on Nov 9. A 0 rating change is less than the 1% target. So the probability is virtually zero, subject to the minimum bound.
2. 2025-12-09: This gives her exactly 1 month / 1 FIDE rating cycle to undergo a 26-point jump. Gaining 26 points in a single month at a ~2560 level is highly improbable and requires heavily outperforming expectations in a dense tournament schedule. 
3. 2026-02-07: Over 3 months, rating variance is slightly higher as she might play multiple events. However, reaching an all-time peak near 2592 (an astronomically high rating for female players historically, approached recently only by a few) remains very unlikely within this brief medium-term window.

Trachoma is caused by Chlamydia trachomatis, and there is currently no approved vaccine. Vaccine development for this pathogen is extremely difficult and no candidates are currently in late-stage (Phase 3) trials that could yield an approval in the next few months. The primary WHO strategy for Trachoma is SAFE (Surgery, Antibiotics, Facial cleanliness, Environmental improvement). Given the short forecast horizon (up to 3 months), a sudden development and approval of a vaccine is biomedically essentially impossible. The probability of the Wikipedia page "List of infectious diseases" listing "Yes" for a Trachoma vaccine on the resolution dates hinges almost entirely on un-reverted vandalism. Because Wikipedia pages are actively monitored, the chance that vandalism occurs and persists exactly on the specific checking dates is negligible. I assign probabilities near the absolute minimum allowed, reflecting the virtually zero base rate for rapid, secret vaccine approval and low probability of persistent Wikipedia vandalism.

The FIDE rankings published on Wikipedia are updated monthly based on FIDE's official releases on the 1st of each month. 
1. For 2025-11-16: This is within the same month as the knowledge cutoff (2025-11-09). The rating on Wikipedia will be identical to the rating on 2025-11-09 (both based on the November FIDE list). Therefore, an increase of at least 1% is virtually impossible (assigning the minimum allowed 0.02).
2. For 2025-12-09: This captures one rating update (December 1). An increase of 1% for a player near 2789 ELO means gaining almost 28 points in a single month. Because the K-factor for top players is 10, gaining 28 points requires outperforming expected scores by 2.8 points in a month, which equates to an almost impossibly dominant tournament performance (e.g. going 7-0 or 8-1 against elite competition). Probability is around 0.03.
3. For 2026-02-07: This captures three monthly updates (Dec, Jan, Feb). Gaining 28 points over three months is more feasible, as it gives the player up to ~30 classical games to rack up wins. Caruana is certainly capable of this (having peaked at 2844), though 28-point leaps at his current level are increasingly rare. The probability is estimated at 0.08. Expected probabilities widen realistically with the time horizon.
